original_abstract: International health research collaborations are increasingly hindered by the stringent requirements of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) regarding data transfers to the United States. Since the GDPR’s inception, numerous high-profile research projects—ranging from cancer epidemiology to COVID-19 therapeutics—have faced significant delays or cancellations. These obstacles are particularly acute for US public and federal institutions, where issues such as sovereign immunity and expansive surveillance laws conflict with EU standards for data subject rights and judicial redress. While the July 2023 EU-US Data Privacy Framework offers a temporary compliance route, its long-term viability remains uncertain given the high probability of legal challenges before the Court of Justice of the EU. This perspective piece analyzes the current legal landscape, evaluates existing transfer mechanisms, and explores how the forthcoming European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation might offer a sustainable path forward. We specifically examine the potential for administrative arrangements between public bodies to bridge the regulatory gap and facilitate essential trans-Atlantic scientific exchange.
humanized_abstract: The EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the future of international health research collaborations The GDPR is causing significant hurdles for the transfer of data for international health research collaborations. Over the years, many international research projects dealing with everything from cancer research to the search for a COVID-19 vaccine have been significantly delayed or scrapped. This impact has, however, disproportionately affected public and federal institutions in the US due to the relationship between the restrictions placed on these institutions by sovereign immunity and surveillance laws and the GDPR's data subject rights and effective judicial remedy protections. Although the Data Privacy Framework agreed between the EU and US in July this year may serve as a temporary solution, it is likely that this decision will be challenged before the EU Court of Justice (CJEU) and its future is therefore uncertain. This column provides a legally orientated overview of current data protection law and the existing mechanisms for transferring personal data to third countries for health research purposes and points out some possible solutions that could be incorporated into the European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation with a view to enabling administrative arrangements between public sector bodies to fill the regulatory void that may otherwise undermine important cross-Atlantic scientific cooperation.

Although the Data Privacy Framework agreed between the EU and US in July this year may serve as a temporary solution, it is likely that this decision will be challenged before the EU Court of Justice (CJEU) and its future is therefore uncertain. This column provides a legally orientated overview of current data protection law and the existing mechanisms for transferring personal data to third countries for health research purposes and points out some possible solutions that could be incorporated into the European Health Data Space (EHDS) regulation with a view to enabling administrative arrangements between public sector bodies to fill the regulatory void that may otherwise undermine important cross-Atlantic scientific cooperation.
